title: Stop hook fails to start on Windows where only 'python' (not 'python3') is on PATH
expected_behavior: After every completed unit, the stop hook (continue-loop.py) evaluates the selector and either continues the invoke loop (exit 2 + decision:block) or stops cleanly (exit 0 + deletes .invoke-active). On continuation, Claude Code runs the next iteration of /invoke-development-harness autonomously.
observed_behavior: On Windows systems where the Python installation exposes only 'python' (not 'python3'), the hook fails to execute. 'env python3' returns 127 ('No such file or directory'). Claude Code's hook machinery treats the failed exec as 'hook did not block' and lets the Stop event complete normally, ending the session. The invoke loop never continues — each unit requires a manual /invoke-development-harness call. Directly running '.claude/hooks/continue-loop.py' from bash on the affected machine reproduces exit 127.
reproduction_steps: Install Python from python.org on Windows (installs 'python' but not 'python3' by default).
Confirm: 'command -v python3' returns nothing; 'command -v python' returns a path.

Bootstrap the harness with /create-development-harness (Claude Code). This writes .claude/settings.local.json with the Stop hook command set to the bare script path '.claude/hooks/continue-loop.py', relying on the script's '#!/usr/bin/env python3' shebang.
Observe: the loop does not auto-continue. '.harness/.invoke-active' remains on disk (not cleaned up because the hook never ran).
Verify the failure mode by running '.claude/hooks/continue-loop.py < /dev/null' in the workspace — expected: exit 127 with 'env: python3: No such file or directory'.
deployment_impact: Not deploy-affecting in the traditional sense (skill repo does not deploy). Impacts every Windows developer who installs the development-harness skill with the default Python layout.
regression_coverage: Add a test fixture that simulates bootstrap on a host where only 'python' (not 'python3') is visible. Assert that the generated .claude/settings.local.json Stop-hook command uses the detected interpreter explicitly, not the bare script path. A unit test around a helper that produces the settings-hook JSON given a detected PY is the cleanest regression guard.
root_cause: During Phase 5 of skills/development-harness/commands/create.md ('Hook configuration'), the settings.local.json is written as a static template where 'command' is the bare script path. That template relies on the script's shebang ('#!/usr/bin/env python3') to locate an interpreter at hook-execution time. Every other entry point in this skill (all 7 workspace command templates + SKILL.md) already uses a portable detection idiom: 'PY=$(command -v python3 2>/dev/null || command -v python 2>/dev/null)'. The hook configuration is the sole place that does not apply this pattern.
fix_approach: In skills/development-harness/commands/create.md Phase 5 ('Hook configuration'), apply the same Python detection the workspace commands use (PY=$(command -v python3 2>/dev/null || command -v python 2>/dev/null)). Write the generated .claude/settings.local.json Stop-hook command as '"$PY" .claude/hooks/continue-loop.py' with the detected path substituted in. Mirror the change in the Cursor .cursor/hooks.json path. The hook script's '#!/usr/bin/env python3' shebang can remain — it is simply no longer the primary invocation path.
raw_text: Stop hook fails to start on Windows systems where python3 is not on PATH but python is. The hook template at skills/development-harness/templates/claude-code/hooks/continue-loop.py uses the shebang env python3 and skills/development-harness/commands/create.md Phase 5 writes the settings.local.json hook command as the bare script path relying on that shebang. When env python3 fails the hook exits 127 and Claude Code treats it as did-not-block and stops normally breaking the invoke loop. Every workspace command template in this skill already uses PY equals command -v python3 or command -v python fallback detection. The fix is to apply the same detection during Phase 5 of create.md and bake the detected interpreter into the settings.local.json command field so the shebang is never relied upon.
